runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 9 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates by income group
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) microsimulation substitution elasticity assumptions
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in a high but not top decile (decile 9), the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation is expected to be modest. Primary earners generally have lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. Decile 9 workers are high-income but not at the very top, where taxable income elasticities can be larger due to avoidance margins. CBO and similar models typically use substitution elasticities for prime-age males/primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.3. Gruber and Saez find compensated elasticities of taxable income around 0.2–0.4 for higher-income groups, but microsimulation hours-based substitution elasticities tend to be somewhat lower. I center my estimate at 0.2, with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and definitions (hours vs. taxable income margin).

(2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation labor supply elasticities"], "reasoning_summary": "For primary earners in a high but not top decile (decile 9), the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation is expected to be modest. CBO and similar models typically use substitution elasticities for prime-age males/primary earners in the range of 0.1\u20130.3. Gruber and Saez find compensated elasticities of taxable income around 0.2\u20130.4 for higher-income groups, but microsimulation hours-based substitution elasticities tend to be somewhat lower. I center my estimate at 0.2, with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and definitions (hours vs. taxable income margin)."}

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the 9th earnings decile (high but not top earners), the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation context is expected to be modest. Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities (extensive margin near zero, intensive margin small). However, at decile 9, there is some scope for hours/effort adjustment and taxable income responses. CBO and similar microsimulation models typically use substitution elasticities in the 0.1–0.3 range for primary earners, with higher-income individuals sometimes assigned slightly higher values reflecting taxable income responses (compensation shifting, deduction timing). Gruber and Saez find elasticities of taxable income around 0.2–0.4 for higher-income groups, but bounded/compensated substitution elasticities used in simulations are typically lower. I center my estimate at 0.20, reflecting a standard parameterization for this decile in U.S. microsimulation work.
